questions.scenario: Fifteen Node services repeat setup-node, npm ci, lint, and test inside jobs that already choose their runner and permissions. The platform team wants to maintain the shared logic as action metadata and shell steps, without introducing a custom JavaScript runtime. The shared block must use the caller's workspace. Which abstraction fits best?

options.text: A reusable workflow that exposes runner and permission inputs so callers can keep their existing job design.
A composite action that packages the repeated steps inside the caller's job.
A local JavaScript action that invokes each setup command through child processes in the caller's job.
A reusable workflow job called by each service, with artifacts used to return the updated workspace.

questions.explanation: A composite action is a step-level boundary and runs inside the caller's job, so it shares the workspace and runner. A reusable workflow still creates a separate job boundary even when it accepts runner inputs, so workspace state needs explicit transfer. A JavaScript action could work but adds runtime code where declarative steps are sufficient. See [Creating a composite action](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/create-actions/create-a-composite-action).

questions.scenario: A composite action includes scripts/summary.sh next to action.yml. A caller checks out checkout-api, invokes the action, and the script lookup incorrectly searches checkout-api/scripts. Which path should the action use?

options.text: The caller's `GITHUB_WORKSPACE`, after checkout places both the service and action files under that directory.
A path supplied by the caller as an action input so each workflow identifies the downloaded action directory.
The current shell directory after the action first changes into its own repository checkout.
github.action_path, which points to the downloaded action package.

questions.explanation: The caller workspace and action package are different directories. `github.action_path` follows the action's own files, while `GITHUB_WORKSPACE` follows the repository being built. Requiring callers to discover an internal path leaks implementation detail, and relying on a prior directory change is less robust than addressing the package directly. See [Composite action metadata syntax](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/metadata-syntax).

questions.scenario: A composite action calculates an image tag that a later caller step must use. The internal shell step currently prints the tag to stdout, but the caller's steps.image.outputs.image-tag is empty. What interface should the action implement?

options.text: Give the internal step an id, write image-tag to GITHUB_OUTPUT, and map that step output in action.yml.
Write the tag to an environment variable through `GITHUB_ENV` and document that later caller steps can read it.
Declare the action output in `action.yml` and rely on the internal step's stdout to populate it.
Write the tag to `GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` and have the caller parse the rendered summary in its next step.

questions.explanation: Outputs are an explicit action interface. The producing step needs an id, writes the named value to `GITHUB_OUTPUT`, and `action.yml` maps it for the caller. `GITHUB_ENV` can share a value inside the same job but bypasses the declared action contract. Stdout and the human-readable step summary do not populate the caller-visible output. See [GitHub action metadata syntax](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/metadata-syntax).

questions.scenario: The platform team centralizes a production deployment as a reusable workflow. It must receive an environment name and a deploy token, but callers should not expose undeclared values. Which interface is correct?

options.text: Declare both values as typed `workflow_call` inputs and mark the deploy token input as required.
Declare the environment as an input and use `secrets: inherit` so every caller secret is available to the deployment job.
Declare a typed workflow_call input and an explicit workflow_call secret, then require callers to map them.
Declare the environment input but read the token from a repository secret with the same name in the called workflow repository.

questions.explanation: `workflow_call` defines a reviewable contract for ordinary values and sensitive values. Treating a token as an input handles it as ordinary data. `secrets: inherit` exposes more secrets than this workflow needs, while a secret owned by the called repository changes the credential source and accountability boundary. See [Reusing workflows](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows).

questions.scenario: Forty production repositories call platform/deploy@main. A merge changes a required input, and every caller's next run fails. Which versioning change best limits the next rollout?

options.text: Keep `main`, but require a release branch and a two-day observation period before merging changes into it.
Publish a reviewed version tag or pin a commit, then let callers upgrade through controlled pull requests.
Publish a major-version branch such as `v2` and move all callers to it automatically after compatibility tests pass.
Pin callers to release tags through an automated dependency update that merges without service review.

questions.explanation: A release tag or commit pin gives callers an intentional upgrade point and keeps the old contract available. Delaying a merge to `main` improves testing but still moves every caller together. A major-version branch is useful only when consumers choose when to adopt it, and automatic unreviewed updates remove the service-level compatibility decision. See [Sharing actions and workflows](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/share-with-your-organization).

questions.scenario: A platform team is reviewing repeated setup, security, and deployment automation across thirty repositories. Which design choices use the right GitHub Actions reuse boundary?

options.text: Package repeated setup and validation steps as a composite action inside the service job.
Use a composite action for the organization security scan so it can select a hardened runner and set job permissions.
Use a reusable workflow for an organization security job with controlled permissions and reporting.
Use a reusable workflow for service-local setup commands that must modify files for later steps in the same caller job.
Keep service-specific values as explicit inputs and shared mechanics behind a versioned interface.

questions.explanation: Composite actions share steps inside a caller job, while reusable workflows own job-level concerns such as runners and permissions. A composite action cannot select its job runner or permissions. A reusable workflow is a poor fit when later caller steps need the same modified workspace. Explicit inputs plus versioned references preserve product ownership. See [Reusing workflows](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/reuse-automations/reuse-workflows).
